So like states like the direct sales state, so Ohio, Utah, Indiana, they were always more responsive for us than any place else. The last two years, we hardly ran in California, and we didn't run on the East Coast at all. We had to retard our media buys back into the places that gave us the biggest bang for the buck. Then, of course, you got to go and search for new lists. So if you were living off five, and now you had to scale back what you were doing in those five, well, there must be another one somewhere. Right? The biggest problem that I'm having with lists right now, and I really want to know how you combat this, is that you're being told that the list is current and everything in it is good. But when you do the mailing, you get large numbers coming back where the addresses aren't good, or that people actually didn't fit what you said. And my question is, how do I eliminate that beforehand, or what questions do I need to ask to solve that problem? I'll say a couple of things. One, are you doing any kind of data hygiene on your list where you're actually cleaning up the list? The Postal Service has software where they can correct addresses, append department numbers, all that good stuff. I'm supposedly paying for that when I buy the list. OK. Are you doing compiled or response-less? I've been doing both. I've been buying some compiled in some response, and I'm getting better results from the response list, but I'm still getting large numbers that are coming back. Well, I'll tell you this. Anytime you do direct mail, right, for years now, you're going to get a lot of mail back no matter what. The Post Office doesn't even know what addresses are deliverable until they show up at the door and say, oh, I guess I can't deliver it. I mean, there's still going to be large numbers. They're not talking 10% or anything like that, but I wouldn't be surprised if it's 4% and 5% of underlevel. The other thing I would say for the response list is get on that list. Call that company, order what they have, and see if your name appears on that list next time you rent it. And you're also going to see all the market information they're sending out. Because if they're a like match for you and your customers, then you might as well be on their list and see what they're mailing anyway, so you can see how to appeal to that group. OK. Yeah, and I'll make another comment that kind of is corollary. So first of all, of course, obviously, there are reputable, less reputable, and disreputable sellers of lists. But the other issue is when you have the ability to use less of it, to narrow it. So people who live in apartments are more transient than people who live in homes. So if you want to reduce, you call out all the apartments. You mail homeowners. People in suburban markets are less transient than people in urban markets. So the surrounding, so if we took Cleveland, where we are not, so Cleveland is north of us and Akron is south of us. The core Cleveland zip codes and the core Akron zip codes are going to have a higher turnover, thus bad deliverables, than is where we are in the middle. People tend to stay put here more than they do in the cities. Affluence is a factor. Again, now it depends on who you want to sell to, but affluence is a factor. The lower the affluence, the more frequently they move, because it's cheaper than paying rent. Certain parts of the country age. So the unavoidable bad that is enlist based on transience is behavioral. And so there's a predictable factor to it out of which you can do selects, even in a B2B environment, so like in professional practices. Now you've got deliverability issues. You've got gatekeeper issues. Well, size of the practice tells you typically how much gatekeeper activity is. So all these things have to be considered in what segment of a list you are going to use. So like with the greenhouse thing you showed. See, generally speaking, there are exceptions, but the fastest way to go broke and direct mail is trying to use the entirety of any list. That's the quickest way to wind up in a poor house. Hardly anybody can ever do it. Were you ever able to do it, like with Ken? You literally were able to use an entire list. Every name they could give you. A bizoplist you could use at all. But not expanding out to other categories. For the most part, you're going to find pieces of a list that can be profitable for you. And you're not going to be able to go beyond those pieces. So I just have to work the list better. I've got to manage the list better. You've got to work the list better. Well, you've got to make sure you're getting stuff from reputable sources, OK? Real list manager companies, real list brokers, not Joe Spadot selling it to you directly out of his basement. The only way you even test those lists is on exchange, swaps, or piece of the action deals you don't pay for them. But so you've got to be dealing with reputable companies. And like he said, you've got to now make sure. So you want to be on the list. You want to see what mail people on the list are getting to see if they're even really mailing what they say they're mailing. You want to look at who's using the list. You showed them that, didn't you? Well, your data cards reveal. Who's on the list? Who's using it? And who's mailing it repeatedly, OK? So did you? We did not talk about this last time. So if I'm going to go rent Al Schwartz opportunity seekers, or I'm going to go rent Smart Practices Dentist buyers, one of the things I want to see is who else is renting the list and who's renting it repeatedly. Because if the names are all unfamiliar to me or they're super big dumb companies where I know nobody knows what the hell they're doing, that makes me nervous. If I'm renting an opportunity seeker list and I see, oh, Ken Roberts is using it. So his clients are using it. George is using it. My confidence goes up because I know they're smart people. So they aren't using this list repeatedly unless it's paying for them. Then the next extension of that, which you may not have shown them, is to do your creative work, creative a bad word, but you understand what I mean. You have content work here. You can go to what used to be inside direct mail. What the hell is it now? I think it's still inside directmail.com. And from the archives, you can buy the control, copies of the controls of the companies who are using the list.
